questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with moderate obstructive sleep apnea (AHI 20) cannot tolerate CPAP despite multiple mask adjustments. He has a BMI of 28 kg/m2 and normal tonsils. Which of the following is the most appropriate alternative therapy?

explanation.summary: Mandibular advancement devices are the recommended alternative for patients with mild to moderate OSA who cannot tolerate CPAP. They advance the mandible forward, enlarging the pharyngeal airway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Oral appliances such as mandibular advancement devices are recommended for patients with mild to moderate OSA who fail CPAP. They work by advancing the mandible and tongue forward, increasing pharyngeal space.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenotonsillectomy is first-line for children with OSA from tonsillar hypertrophy. This adult has normal tonsils.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BiPAP provides bilevel pressures for central sleep apnea or patients requiring higher inspiratory pressures, not for CPAP mask intolerance in OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypoglossal nerve stimulation is emerging therapy for selected patients who fail CPAP. An oral appliance trial is typically first.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tracheostomy is a last-resort treatment. It is not appropriate for moderate OSA with available alternatives.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with untreated obstructive sleep apnea presents to the emergency department after a motor vehicle accident. He reports falling asleep at the wheel. His wife has been urging him to seek treatment for loud snoring. Which of the following is this event an example of?

explanation.summary: Untreated OSA causes fragmented sleep from recurrent arousals, leading to excessive daytime sleepiness that significantly increases motor vehicle accident risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excessive daytime sleepiness is a major consequence of fragmented sleep in untreated OSA. Patients have significantly increased risk of drowsy driving and motor vehicle accidents.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While OSA is associated with arrhythmias, the patient reports falling asleep, consistent with excessive daytime sleepiness rather than syncope.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypoxia-induced seizures would present with convulsive activity, not gradual sleep onset.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Narcolepsy is a separate disorder with cataplexy and sleep paralysis. This patient's sleepiness is from untreated OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: TIAs cause focal neurological deficits, not gradual sleep onset while driving.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with OSA treated with CPAP for 3 years presents for follow-up. His wife reports resolved snoring and no apneic episodes. However, he has persistent morning headaches. An arterial blood gas shows PaCO2 of 50 mm Hg while awake. His BMI is 38 kg/m2. Which of the following best explains his persistent symptoms?

explanation.summary: Morning headaches and daytime hypercapnia despite well-controlled OSA suggest coexisting obesity hypoventilation syndrome, characterized by obesity with chronic daytime hypercapnia not explained by another condition.
explanation.whyCorrect: OHS is defined by obesity (BMI >=30), chronic awake hypoventilation (PaCO2 >45 mm Hg), and sleep-disordered breathing. It frequently coexists with OSA. Despite adequate CPAP controlling obstructive events, the chronic hypercapnia and morning headaches indicate OHS requiring additional management.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CPAP malfunction would result in recurrent obstructive events and snoring. The wife confirms these are resolved.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While CSA can coexist with OSA, awake hypercapnia is more consistent with OHS than isolated CSA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inadequate CPAP pressure would fail to resolve snoring and apneas, which are reported as resolved.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Morning headaches with awake hypercapnia are due to CO2 retention causing cerebral vasodilation, not coincidental tension headache.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man is referred for a sleep study. He has a BMI of 32 kg/m2 and reports loud snoring. The study can be done in a sleep laboratory or at home. Which of the following measurements is available with in-laboratory polysomnography but typically NOT with home sleep testing?

explanation.summary: Home sleep apnea testing devices typically do not include EEG, meaning they cannot reliably detect arousals or determine sleep stages, unlike in-laboratory polysomnography.
explanation.whyCorrect: In-laboratory PSG includes EEG monitoring for sleep staging and arousal detection. Most home devices lack EEG, so they cannot identify sleep stages or arousals, potentially underestimating the AHI since total sleep time cannot be accurately determined.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Airflow measurement is a core component of both in-laboratory PSG and home devices.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heart rate monitoring is included in both settings through pulse oximetry or ECG.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oxygen saturation via pulse oximetry is standard in both in-laboratory and home testing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Many home devices include respiratory effort bands, though quality may vary. EEG is the major absent modality.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with atrial fibrillation and congestive heart failure presents with worsening daytime sleepiness. His wife describes progressively deeper breathing, then shallower, followed by a pause, in a repeating cycle throughout the night. Which pathophysiological mechanism best explains this pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pharyngeal muscle collapse during REM sleep

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed chemoreceptor feedback from prolonged circulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diaphragmatic fatigue from chronic heart failure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vagal nerve stimulation from atrial distension

explanation.summary: In heart failure, prolonged circulatory time delays blood gas information from reaching central chemoreceptors, creating an overshoot-undershoot ventilatory pattern manifesting as Cheyne-Stokes respiration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heart failure causes prolonged circulation time, so changes in blood gas tensions take longer to reach brainstem chemoreceptors. This delay creates feedback loop instability: by the time the brain detects rising PaCO2, hyperventilation overshoots, dropping PaCO2 below the apneic threshold and causing apnea. The cycle repeats.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pharyngeal muscle collapse is the mechanism of OSA. This patient has a crescendo-decrescendo pattern consistent with central sleep apnea.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diaphragmatic fatigue does not produce cyclical crescendo-decrescendo breathing. CSA with heart failure is due to ventilatory control instability.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The key mechanism involves central CO2 chemoreceptors with delayed feedback, not peripheral oxygen chemoreceptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While atrial distension occurs in heart failure, vagal stimulation does not cause periodic breathing. The mechanism is delayed circulatory feedback.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with severe OSA (AHI 45) has been on CPAP for 6 months but removes it after 2 hours nightly. He drinks 3 beers every evening and takes lorazepam for anxiety before bed. Which recommendation is most likely to improve his sleep apnea symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add supplemental oxygen to CPAP circuit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discontinue alcohol and sedatives before sleep

explanation.summary: Alcohol and benzodiazepines decrease pharyngeal muscle tone and suppress arousal responses, worsening OSA. Discontinuing these substances before sleep is a critical lifestyle modification.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both alcohol and benzodiazepines worsen OSA by decreasing upper airway muscle tone, promoting pharyngeal collapse, and increasing the arousal threshold. Discontinuing these is a key intervention.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Supplemental oxygen does not address upper airway obstruction. Addressing modifiable factors is the priority.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Increasing pressure does not compensate for limited use duration. Addressing alcohol and sedatives that worsen OSA is the priority.
whyWrongByChoice.C: With severe OSA (AHI 45), CPAP remains preferred. Addressing modifiable worsening factors should come first.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While auto-titrating CPAP may improve comfort, addressing alcohol and benzodiazepines that actively worsen OSA should be the first step.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with obstructive sleep apnea undergoes echocardiogram showing right ventricular dilation, tricuspid regurgitation, and estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 55 mm Hg. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiological link between his OSA and these cardiac findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic intermittent hypoxia causing pulmonary vasoconstriction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left ventricular diastolic dysfunction from obesity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nocturnal hypertension causing aortic root dilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recurrent Valsalva maneuvers increasing right atrial pressure

explanation.summary: Chronic intermittent hypoxia from recurrent obstructive apneas causes hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, leading over time to pulmonary hypertension, right ventricular dilation, and cor pulmonale.
explanation.whyCorrect: Recurrent hypoxia triggers pulmonary arteriolar vasoconstriction. Chronic exposure leads to vascular remodeling and sustained pulmonary hypertension. Increased RV afterload results in dilation and tricuspid regurgitation (cor pulmonale).

whyWrongByChoice.B: While obesity commonly coexists with OSA, the direct link between OSA and right heart findings is through pulmonary vasoconstriction from intermittent hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: OSA causes nocturnal BP surges, but right heart findings are from pulmonary hypertension, not aortic root pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Negative intrathoracic pressure swings occur during obstructive events but are not Valsalva maneuvers. The primary mechanism is pulmonary hypertension from chronic hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The tricuspid regurgitation is functional from RV dilation, not structural valve damage from inflammation.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with a BMI of 26 kg/m2 presents with excessive daytime sleepiness. Physical examination reveals a small, posteriorly displaced mandible. Her husband reports loud snoring. Polysomnography reveals an AHI of 12. Which anatomical finding most directly contributes to her condition?

explanation.summary: Retrognathia (posteriorly displaced mandible) narrows the retroglossal airway space, predisposing to pharyngeal collapse during sleep. This is a recognized structural risk factor for OSA, especially in non-obese patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Retrognathia causes posterior displacement of the mandible and tongue base, reducing retroglossal airway space and predisposing to upper airway collapse during sleep. In this non-obese patient, the small, posteriorly displaced mandible is the most likely anatomical contributor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A deviated nasal septum can worsen snoring but is not the primary mechanism causing pharyngeal collapse in a patient with documented retrognathia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An enlarged thyroid can cause external airway compression but is not described in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Macroglossia can contribute to obstruction, but the examination specifically identifies retrognathia as the structural abnormality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tonsillar hypertrophy is the most common cause in children. This patient has retrognathia identified on exam, with no enlarged tonsils mentioned.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with hypothyroidism and a BMI of 36 kg/m2 is diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnea. He asks his physician why sleeping on his back makes symptoms worse. Which of the following best explains the positional worsening?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased chest wall compliance when supine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduced diaphragmatic excursion from abdominal contents

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gravity displaces tongue posteriorly when supine

explanation.summary: In the supine position, gravity pulls the tongue and soft palate posteriorly toward the pharyngeal wall, narrowing the upper airway and increasing the likelihood of obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: When sleeping supine, gravity causes the tongue, soft palate, and pharyngeal tissues to fall posteriorly, narrowing the already compromised upper airway. This is why positional therapy (avoiding supine sleep) can be beneficial.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Venous return actually increases in the supine position. This is not the mechanism for OSA worsening.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Chest wall compliance is not significantly altered by position. The worsening is due to gravitational effects on upper airway soft tissues.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While abdominal contents can reduce diaphragmatic excursion in obese patients, the specific positional worsening of OSA is from posterior tongue displacement.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The vagus nerve does not compress the airway. Positional worsening is mechanical from gravity on pharyngeal tissues.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with acromegaly presents for evaluation of excessive daytime sleepiness and witnessed apneas. Her BMI is 27 kg/m2. Polysomnography confirms OSA with an AHI of 28. Which feature of acromegaly most directly contributes to her obstructive sleep apnea?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Growth hormone excess causing insulin resistance

explanation.summary: Acromegaly causes macroglossia and pharyngeal tissue enlargement from excess growth hormone, narrowing the upper airway and predisposing to obstructive sleep apnea.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excess growth hormone causes soft tissue hypertrophy throughout the body, including macroglossia and pharyngeal tissue enlargement. These narrow the upper airway, making it more susceptible to collapse during sleep.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While acromegaly can cause cardiomyopathy, fluid redistribution is not the primary mechanism of OSA. The cause is soft tissue hypertrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Insulin resistance contributes to metabolic complications but does not directly cause upper airway obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Peripheral neuropathy would contribute to central hypoventilation, not obstructive sleep apnea from anatomical narrowing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The pituitary gland is in the sella turcica, far from the brainstem. A pituitary adenoma does not compress the brainstem.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with severe heart failure and central sleep apnea continues to have an elevated AHI with predominantly central events despite optimized heart failure medications and a trial of CPAP. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refer for phrenic nerve stimulator placement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Switch to bilevel positive airway pressure

explanation.summary: For CSA patients with heart failure who fail CPAP despite optimized cardiac therapy, bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) provides higher inspiratory pressures to support ventilation during central apneas.
explanation.whyCorrect: When CSA persists despite optimized heart failure therapy and CPAP trial, BiPAP is an appropriate escalation. BiPAP delivers higher inspiratory and lower expiratory pressures, more effectively supporting ventilation when central respiratory drive is insufficient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Supplemental oxygen alone does not adequately address central apneas or provide ventilatory support during absent respiratory drive.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Discontinuing positive airway pressure would worsen hypoxemia and sleep quality.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Phrenic nerve stimulation is emerging therapy but not yet standard of care. BiPAP should be trialed first.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty treats obstructive sleep apnea by removing pharyngeal tissue. It has no role in central sleep apnea.
